title: Subscription Cascade Attack with Sequential Trial Abuse and Mixed MCCs
narrative: Fraudster exploits a compromised card by rapidly enrolling in 8-15 subscription services within 45-90 minutes, focusing on low-value trial periods ($0.99-$9.99) across streaming, fitness, gaming, and SaaS platforms. Attack begins with 2-3 legitimate-appearing subscriptions at recognized merchants (MCC 5815, 7372), then accelerates to obscure subscription boxes and niche services. Each transaction uses identical card1 but rotates through 3-4 P_emaildomains (temporary email services like guerrillamail, 10minutemail) to bypass merchant fraud filters. Fraudster exploits the 24-48 hour settlement window before victim notices, knowing most subscriptions won't bill full amount until day 7-30. TransactionAmt stays under $15 to avoid velocity thresholds, but aggregate hourly spend reaches $80-120. Pattern terminates abruptly after final high-value annual subscription ($79.99-$199.99) at productivity software merchant.
indicators: card1_txn_count_1hr between 8-15 with ProductCD='W' (subscription/digital goods) exclusively
P_emaildomain shows 3+ distinct temporary email providers within single card1 session
TransactionAmt standard deviation <$8 for first 70% of transactions, then spike to $79-$199
MCC diversity: 4+ different subscription categories (5815, 7372, 7995, 5968) in under 90 minutes
card1_addr1_nunique=1 but P_emaildomain_nunique â‰¥3, indicating email rotation on static billing address
dist1 (distance between billing/IP) remains consistent <50km while email domains change
Final transaction TransactionAmt exceeds median of prior transactions by 8-15x within same session
summary: A card rapidly signs up for 8-15 subscriptions in 45-90 minutes, using the same billing details but rotating through 3+ temporary email domains. Transaction amounts stay under $15 until a final spike to $79-$199 for an annual subscription.
reasoning: The fraudster tests a stolen card across multiple low-value trials to avoid detection, using disposable emails to bypass merchant controls. The abrupt termination after one high-value annual charge shows intent to maximize extraction before the victim detects unauthorized activity.
indicator_explanations: Card hits subscription services repeatedly in a concentrated hour-long window
Email address switches between multiple disposable providers during the spree
Small consistent charges suddenly jump to much larger amounts late in session
Card hits four different subscription merchant types in rapid succession
Billing address stays fixed while email rotates through different providers
Physical location remains constant despite changing email domains across transactions
Final purchase amount explodes to ten times the earlier transaction sizes
